body:
## Problem

cmdwin (the `q:` cmdline buffer) has various limitations which require [special-casing](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40077) all over the codebase.

Besides complicating the code, it also breaks async plugins, which often asynchronously create buffers/windows after some work is done.
If the user happens to open cmdwin in the moment of such an async handler, the plugin cannot proceed:

    Lua callback: …/guh.nvim/lua/guh/util.lua:531:
    E11: Invalid in command-line window; <CR> executes, CTRL-C quits
    stack traceback:
        [C]: in function 'nvim_buf_delete'
        …/guh.nvim/lua/guh/util.lua:531: in function <…/guh.nvim/lua/guh/util.lua:526>

then plugins have to do workarounds like:

<details>
<summary>workaround example</summary>


      --- Deletes `buf`, deferring if the command-line window (`q:`/`q/`) is open.
      function M.buf_delete_safe(buf)
        if vim.fn.getcmdwintype() ~= '' then
          vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd('CmdwinLeave', {
            once = true,
            callback = function()
              vim.schedule(function()
                if vim.api.nvim_buf_is_valid(buf) then
                  vim.api.nvim_buf_delete(buf, { force = true })
                end
              end)
            end,
          })
          return
        end
        vim.api.nvim_buf_delete(buf, { force = true })
      end


</details>

## Expected behavior

Reimplement cmdwin as a normal buffer+window.

All of the cmdwin contortions (in both core, and innocent plugins) exist literally only to support "inception": recursive cmdwin-in-cmdline-things, like `<c-r>=`, `/`, search-during-substitute, `:input()`, etc. So we just won't support that (though I have a potential plan for that later, which I call "modal parking lot").

The benefit is that plugins, and core, no longer have to care about cmdwin.

---

## Analysis  (AI-assisted)

### Why cmdwin is "blocking" today

When you enter cmdwin, `getcmdline_int` calls into `open_cmdwin`, which spins up a nested `state_enter` loop. The outer cmdline call is suspended; the nested loop runs normal-mode against a scratchbuf until `<Enter>` sets `cmdwin_result` and exits the loop. Then `getcmdline_int` resumes and treats the returned string as if the user had typed it.

### Arguments FOR blocking-cmdwin

1. The cmdline reader has nontrivial state on its stack: partial buffer, cursor pos, history index, wildmenu/completion state, the prompt char. Whereas "async" = serialize and rehydrate all of it.
2. Trivial reentrancy story.  Whereas with "async", every code path that asks "is the user editing a command line?" needs a new tri-state answer.

### Arguments AGAINST blocking-cmdwin

1. Every "what mode am I in" check has to special-case cmdwin. [#40077](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/40077) (800 lines) exists because inccommand can't see the cmdwin's edits as edits to `:`. Same story for LSP-on-cmdline, completion popups, vim.on_key, autocommands. Each workaround is more code.
2. cmdwin is interactive-only, it **doesn't actually support macros/mappings ??** Nested `state_enter` was maybe relevant when Ex-mode needed to read input mid-stream during macro playback (`input()`, `confirm()`, `getchar()`).
3. The atomic-return advantage is illusory. "Cmdwin returns a string to the cmdline reader" is what `feedkeys()` + a `BufWinLeave` autocmd already does today in user-land.
    - TODO: `vim.schedule_dispatch` ? To schedule functions in the input queue (as opposed to `vim.schedule`).
